messages.content: flutter tabbar indicator disappeared after hot reload

There could be multiple reasons why the tabbar indicator disappeared after hot reload in Flutter. Here are a few possible solutions:

1. Check if the indicator color is set to transparent: Make sure that the color set for the tabbar indicator is not transparent. If it is, then that could be the reason why the indicator is not visible.

2. Check if the indicator size is set to zero: If the size of the indicator is set to zero or a very small value, then it might not be visible. Make sure that the size is large enough for the indicator to be visible.

3. Check if the indicator's position is set correctly: The indicator's position should be set according to the selected tab. If the position is not set correctly, then the indicator might not be visible.

4. Restart the app: If none of the above solutions work, try restarting the app. Sometimes, a hot reload may not work as expected, and a full app restart might be required to fix the issue.

5. Check for any updates: Make sure that you are using the latest version of Flutter and that all packages used in the project are up-to-date. Sometimes, an outdated package might cause issues like this.
flutter tabbar indicator disappeared after hot reload, restart works fine

If the tabbar indicator disappeared after hot reload, but restarting the app works fine, then it is likely that the hot reload did not update the UI elements properly. This can happen if there are changes to the UI elements that are not picked up by the hot reload process. 

In this case, restarting the app completely will ensure that all the UI elements are updated properly. It is a good practice to test changes thoroughly after a hot reload to make sure that they are working as expected. If there are any issues, a full restart of the app should be done to ensure that the changes are applied properly.

In general, it is recommended to use hot reload as a development aid rather than a replacement for testing changes. While hot reload can speed up the development process by allowing developers to see changes in real-time, it is not a replacement for proper testing.
flutter tabbar isScrollable meaning

In Flutter, the `TabBar` widget can be set to either scrollable or fixed mode. When `isScrollable` is set to true, it means that the tabs in the `TabBar` will be scrollable horizontally, allowing the user to swipe left or right to see more tabs if there are more than can fit on the screen.

In this mode, the tabs will be sized to fit their label text, with a minimum width of 48 pixels. If the text is longer than the available width, the text will be truncated with an ellipsis.

When `isScrollable` is set to false (the default), it means that the tabs will be fixed in size and will not be scrollable. If there are more tabs than can fit on the screen, the extra tabs will be hidden and the user will not be able to see or select them.

In fixed mode, the tabs will be evenly sized based on the available width of the `TabBar`. This can result in labels being truncated if the text is longer than the available space.

In general, it is recommended to use `isScrollable` when there are more tabs than can fit on the screen to prevent overcrowding and make it easier for the user to navigate between tabs.
flutter tabbar isScrollable true indicator lost after hot reload

If the tabbar indicator is lost after a hot reload in Flutter, it could be due to a couple of reasons. One possible reason is that the `isScrollable` property is not set correctly after the hot reload. 

To fix this issue, you can try re-applying the `isScrollable` property on the `TabBar` widget after the hot reload. If the property is set correctly, the indicator should appear again. 

Another possible reason for losing the tabbar indicator after a hot reload is that the `indicatorColor` or `indicatorWeight` properties were not set correctly on the `TabBar` widget. Make sure that the `indicatorColor` and `indicatorWeight` properties are set to the desired values after a hot reload, as these properties determine the color and thickness of the indicator.

If these solutions do not work, try doing a full restart of the app. Sometimes, hot reload may not be enough to correct UI issues, and a full restart is needed to ensure that all UI elements are properly updated.
flutter tabbar how to get fixed tab width when isScrollable false

In Flutter, when the `isScrollable` property of a `TabBar` widget is set to false, the tabs will be fixed in size and evenly sized based on the available width of the `TabBar`. This can result in the text being truncated if the text is longer than the available space.

To get a fixed tab width with `isScrollable` set to false, you can use the `TabBar` widget inside a `SizedBox`, and set the `width` property of the `SizedBox` to the desired width. This will create a fixed width `TabBar` that will display all the tabs without truncating the text.

Here's an example of how to create a fixed width tabbar:

```
SizedBox(
  width: 300.0, // set the desired width
  child: TabBar(
    isScrollable: false,
    tabs: [
      Tab(text: 'Tab 1'),
      Tab(text: 'Tab 2'),
      Tab(text: 'Tab 3'),
    ],
  ),
)
```

In this example, the `SizedBox` widget is used to create a container with a fixed width of `300.0` pixels. The `TabBar` widget is then placed inside the `SizedBox`, and the `isScrollable` property is set to false. This will create a fixed width `TabBar` that displays all the tabs without truncating the text.
flutter tabbar isScrollable true make tabs align left

In Flutter, when the `isScrollable` property of a `TabBar` widget is set to true, the tabs will be scrollable horizontally, and can be aligned in a few different ways. By default, the tabs will be centered horizontally, but you can align the tabs to the left by wrapping the `TabBar` widget in a `Container` or `Row` widget and setting the `alignment` property to `Alignment.centerLeft`.

Here's an example of how to align the tabs of a scrollable `TabBar` to the left:

```
Container(
  alignment: Alignment.centerLeft,
  child: TabBar(
    isScrollable: true,
    tabs: [
      Tab(text: 'Tab 1'),
      Tab(text: 'Tab 2'),
      Tab(text: 'Tab 3'),
      Tab(text: 'Tab 4'),
      Tab(text: 'Tab 5'),
      Tab(text: 'Tab 6'),
    ],
  ),
),
```

In this example, the `Container` widget is used to wrap the `TabBar` widget, and the `alignment` property is set to `Alignment.centerLeft`. This will align the tabs to the left of the `Container`.

You can also use a `Row` widget with the `mainAxisAlignment` property set to `MainAxisAlignment.start` to achieve the same effect:

```
Row(
  mainAxisAlignment: MainAxisAlignment.start,
  children: [
    Expanded(
      child: TabBar(
        isScrollable: true,
        tabs: [
          Tab(text: 'Tab 1'),
          Tab(text: 'Tab 2'),
          Tab(text: 'Tab 3'),
          Tab(text: 'Tab 4'),
          Tab(text: 'Tab 5'),
          Tab(text: 'Tab 6'),
        ],
      ),
    ),
  ],
),
```

In this example, the `Row` widget is used to wrap the `TabBar` widget, and the `mainAxisAlignment` property is set to `MainAxisAlignment.start`. This will also align the tabs to the left of the `Row`.
flutter tabbar isScrollable false make tabs align left

In Flutter, when the `isScrollable` property of a `TabBar` widget is set to false, the tabs are centered by default. To align the tabs to the left in a `TabBar` with `isScrollable` set to false, you can use a `Row` widget with the `mainAxisSize` set to `MainAxisSize.min`, which will align the children to the start of the main axis.

Here's an example of how to align the tabs of a `TabBar` with `isScrollable` set to false:

```
Row(
  mainAxisSize: MainAxisSize.min,
  children: [
    Tab(
      text: 'Tab 1',
    ),
    Tab(
      text: 'Tab 2',
    ),
    Tab(
      text: 'Tab 3',
    ),
  ],
)
```

In this example, a `Row` widget is used to wrap the `Tab` widgets, and the `mainAxisSize` is set to `MainAxisSize.min` to align the children to the start of the main axis. The `Tab` widgets will be evenly sized based on the available width of the `Row`, and the text will not be truncated.

You can also use a `ListView` or `Wrap` widget to create a scrollable `TabBar` with left-aligned tabs. For example, using a `Wrap` widget:

```
Wrap(
  children: [
    Tab(
      text: 'Tab 1',
    ),
    Tab(
      text: 'Tab 2',
    ),
    Tab(
      text: 'Tab 3',
    ),
    Tab(
      text: 'Tab 4',
    ),
    Tab(
      text: 'Tab 5',
    ),
    Tab(
      text: 'Tab 6',
    ),
  ],
),
```

In this example, a `Wrap` widget is used to wrap the `Tab` widgets. The `Wrap` widget will automatically wrap the `Tab` widgets onto multiple lines if there are too many to fit in a single row. The `Tab` widgets will be arranged left-to-right, and will be evenly sized based on the available width.

perforce depot local stream

In Perforce, a stream depot is a tree-like structure that represents the development of a project over time. Each stream depot contains its own branch of the project codebase, with its own set of files and change history. 

To create a local stream depot in Perforce, you can use the `p4 stream` command. First, navigate to your workspace directory in the command-line interface, and then run the following command:

```
p4 stream -t <parent-depot>/parent-stream <local-depot>/<local-stream>
```

In this command, `<parent-depot>/parent-stream` refers to the parent stream depot that the local stream depot will be based on, and `<local-depot>/<local-stream>` refers to the name of the local stream depot that you want to create.

For example, if you want to create a local stream depot called `my-local-stream` based on the parent depot `//depot/main` and the parent stream `main`, you could use the following command:

```
p4 stream -t //depot/main/main my-local-depot/my-local-stream
```

After running this command, Perforce will create a new stream depot called `my-local-stream` in the `my-local-depot` directory of your workspace. You can then use this depot to manage the development of your project, using the same Perforce commands and tools that you would use with a standard depot.
difference between local and stream in perforce

In Perforce, a local depot and a stream depot are two different ways of organizing and managing your project files and development history.

A local depot is a simple repository where you can store files on your local machine. Local depots are typically used for small projects or for temporary storage of files that are not ready to be checked into a central repository. Local depots can be easily created and deleted using the `p4 depot` command.

A stream depot, on the other hand, is a specialized type of depot that is used to manage multiple branches or development streams of a project. Stream depots use a hierarchical structure to organize the different streams, with each stream having its own set of files and development history. The history of changes to files in a stream depot is automatically tracked and recorded, enabling you to see how the project has evolved over time.

Stream depots provide advanced features for managing parallel development streams, including merge tracking, branch visualization, and automatic conflict resolution. Stream depots also make it easier to manage development on multiple platforms and integrate changes from multiple developers into a single codebase.

In summary, a local depot is a simple repository for storing files on your local machine, while a stream depot is a specialized type of depot that is used to manage multiple branches or development streams of a project. Stream depots provide advanced features for managing parallel development streams, while local depots are best suited for small or temporary projects.
